> I do wonder if there's actually value in delaying the construction of the aper._illuminated attr,

The `_illuminated` construction used `gsparams` values, so delaying it meant that it doesn't waste calculations that might get thrown away if this is combined into a Convolve with a different gsparams.

Thanks to some hints from Josh, I found that 45 of the 60 seconds come from the ```calculate_maxk``` step when it initializes the final InterpolatedImage.  This would suggest allowing users to pass in a maxk value in cases where they know that they are going to convolve with something else that has a smaller maxk than the optical PSF might have.

So I tried that.  Good news: the time to initialize shrank to ~15 seconds as expected.  (Next job: figure out what that 15 seconds is.)  Bad news: the time to **draw** the convolution of the optical and atmospheric PSF inflated from well below a second to 40 seconds.  That's because the FFT of the InterpolatedImage is only done once, either when you calculate maxK or otherwise, so it has to happen sometime.

Conclusion: we should downsample the file that Aaron sent; perhaps we can get nearly the same results without having ~16 pixels across each spider.  I tried downsampling from 2048^2 to 512^2 and even 256^2, in the spirit of living dangerously.  256^2 raised a warning that it wasn't sampled enough even for a simple unobscured Airy, so forget that.  512^2 is nominally fine - at first - and time to initialize drops to 3 seconds from 60 seconds.  For reference, the high resolution optical PSF image (2048^2 pupil plane, 0.02 arcsec pixel scale):
![real_spider_final_test_10](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20146936/e34fc4ba-a673-11e6-88e1-3fd7ba2bb90a.png)
While for 512^2 it's 
![real_spider_final_test_11](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20146944/ea586bd6-a673-11e6-9fd4-17fc85251c5b.png)
I was going to suggest that we go with 512^2 as the default, since it clearly gives almost exactly correct diffraction features much more quickly (and then have a "highres" option for those who really care about all the details).

But... when we convolve with a Kolmogorov with FWHM=0.65 arcsec and draw into an image with 0.2 arcsec pixels, something funny happens.  After using 2048^2 pupil plane image:
![real_spider_final_test_kolm_10](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20146983/16946a06-a674-11e6-84be-fd4db13c0f34.png)
After using 512^2 pupil plane image:
![real_spider_final_test_kolm_11](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1454906/20146991/1bd01f9c-a674-11e6-8f1d-d2b4eb8c29a5.png)
You see a funny box feature.  The origin of the feature is that when we use the lower res pupil plane image, that sets a maximum physical extent of the interpolated image stored inside the OpticalPSF - 3 arcsec or so.  But if you are convolving with a reasonable atmospheric PSF, the natural image size is larger than that.  So you get a funny box feature that is not easily removed by changing the OpticalPSF kwargs like gsparams, oversampling, or pad factor.

Then I came up with plan B:
If the high-res optical PSF is very expensive to initialize but not nearly as expensive to use, we could cache the results of the initialization step in share/ and then the LSST PSF routine could use the cached things (unless different oversampling etc. are requested).

That also doesn't work for two reasons:
1 - the stuff to be cached would also depend on the aberrations, which presumably will differ for different calls
2 - Josh pointed out that the stuff to be cached depends on wavelength

I'm stumped.  Anyone else have suggestions?

I've thought about this a bit more, and I think it could be enough to provide component fallbacks then let people in complete control over the handling and potential logging. 

There would be:

- `block_fallback` with access to block type, data, and depth (and its children)
- `style_fallback` with access to style type (and its children)
- `entity_fallback` with access to entity type, data (and its children)

If one of those is specified, the related exceptions would simply not be raised. Then, any logging or replacement behaviour can be handled with those fallbacks. "it's just functions", which allows more flexibility, and doesn't require adding too much logging code in the exporter itself.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import os
import sys
import time
import cPickle

from manticore import Manticore

saved_state = 'just_after_main.state'

MAIN_ADDR = int(sys.argv[2], 16)
start_time = time.time()
m = None
if os.path.exists(saved_state):
    with open(saved_state, 'rb') as f:
        m = Manticore(cPickle.load(f))
else:
    m = Manticore(sys.argv[1])


@m.hook(MAIN_ADDR)
def log_msg(state):
    print "Reached main after %f seconds" % (time.time() - start_time)
    with open(saved_state, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(cPickle.dumps(state, 2))
    m.terminate()

m.run(procs=1)
```

@tseaver Regarding your doubt:

-   `test_list_files` only has one assertion and `list(self.bucket.list_blobs())` is definitely subject to eventual consistency (it may have to hit multiple pages, but more likely a single page)
-   `test_paginate_files` (using `max_results=2`): The assertions are
    1.  2 items in first page (can fail if backend's list only has 1 element due to eventual consistentcy)
    1.  `iterator` is on the first page (can't fail, just a `+= 1` locally)
    1.  `next_page_token` isn't null in the first page / i.e. there is another page (can fail if backend's list only has 2 elements due to eventual consistency)
    1.  the number of blobs in the last page is 1 (probably can't fail if the previous succeeded, though might if two different machines had different version of state)
-   `test_root_level_w_delimiter` gets all prefixes and files not containing `/`. The assertions are:
    1.  the only blobs returned are `file01.txt` (can fail if backend doesn't have `file01.txt` due to eventual consistency)
    1.  `iterator` is on the first page (can't fail, just a `+= 1` locally)
    1.  `next_page_token` is null / i.e. there is another page (probably can't fail?)
    1.  the prefixes returned are `parent/` (can fail if backend doesn't have any of the `parent/` files due to eventual consistency)
-   `test_first_level` gets all prefixes and files not containing `/` with prefix `prefix/`. The assertions are:
    1.  the only blobs returned are `parent/file11.txt` (can fail if backend doesn't have `parent/file11.txt` due to eventual consistency)
    1.  `iterator` is on the first page (can't fail, just a `+= 1` locally)
    1.  `next_page_token` is null / i.e. there is another page (probably can't fail?)
    1.  the prefixes returned are `parent/child/` (can fail if backend doesn't have any of the `parent/child/` files due to eventual consistency)

`test_second_level` and `test_third_level` are essentially the same as `test_first_level`

That's correct; this PR does have a breaking change that would force people to update their templates. I went this route for two reasons:

1. The current UpdatePolicy class only supports a subset of the CFN UpdatePolicy attribute's capabilities. The new(er) `policies.UpdatePolicy` class supports the full breadth of the CFN attribute and is written in such a way to support future changes whereas the old UpdatePolicy cannot.
2. I didn't want to have two separate UpdatePolicy classes as that would only lead to confusion and more complex validation code since we would need to check what instance of UpdatePolicy someone is using. I think we should just have a single way to do things; the more flexible and complete of the two is the obvious choice.

I assumed making an API-breaking change would be acceptable due to the pre-1.0 version and the upgrade path is straightforward as well: https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/pull/173/files#diff-499e3b7a9d5227fa78ded8dcb7e1ee2aL228

The goal here is to have something that'll work for all the major uses of multi-process instrumentation in Python, with semantics that are as close as we can get them to the threaded equivalent.

I'm not too familiar with uWSGI's cache. My initial research indicates that it's not a good match. Firstly, it's a cache not a datastore so we need to worry about expiry. Secondly it appears to support modes meant for multi-machine synchronisation including UDP and Django's database. If a user is using one of these then the semantics and performance will not be as desired. Finally it only works for those using uWSGI so even if we can handle the previous issues, we're left with all the other use cases.

We do need to support floats. As it can only store a 64bit integer and we're using 64bit floats, one approach is to put the float's machine representation in the cache.

At a high level I think this could be an option for some of our users, but it's not a full solution for the multi-process problem. As it stands the only thing preventing #66 being a full solution is getting a shelve equivalent that support concurrency and that doesn't call fsync on every write to disk.

Hi Benjamin, I've merged the Bookmarks sequence acceptance tests with some
changes. Basically I combined the three sequence aspects into a single
scenario and placed it in a new bookmarks feature; this feature is distinct
enough to warrant its own scenario and step files.

The first WIP-acceptance-test error drives the next TDD step, as always, and
in this case it's: `TypeError: object of type 'Bookmarks' has no len()` which
of course points us to the `Bookmarks.__len__()` implementation.

I looked at your implementation, and I think we're going to need something
more sophisticated in this case. `len(bookmarks)` should not count open
bookmarks (including out-of-sequence start-end pairs) or bookmarks with
duplicate names. Any other exception rules we uncover need to be excluded
from the count too.

So I'm thinking this prods us to think strategically about how we'll provide
sequence behaviors and also puts us into tension with a clean division of
responsibility between the `Bookmarks` sequence object and the individual
`Bookmark` objects it contains.

Here are a few initial impressions I have:

* I believe we are forced to gather every well-formed bookmark in the story
  on every sequence call (len, getitem, iter).

  One of the subtleties of working with the XML is that no state can be
  stored in the proxy object (e.g. Bookmark, Paragraph, etc.); the state of
  the document must _only_ be stored in the XML, lest the proxy objects
  become out of sync with the XML, which is what is actually stored on save.

  It follows then that we cannot cache the bookmarks; we have no way of
  knowing whether the user is manipulating them in some other way, perhaps
  with another Bookmarks object stored in a variable.

* I'm thinking this retrieve-well-formed-bookmarks should be done at the oxml
  layer and _not_ by calling `Bookmark` proxy objects into that role.

  The first reason is that oxml-level performance is mostly C-speed, so
  I expect it would perform better.

  The second reason is that it would complicate `Bookmark` to give it
  a retrieval role it otherwise shouldn't need.

* I'm not entirely clear how to manage this, but my best working theory is to
  form an XPath expression that gets _all_ bookmarkStart and bookmarkEnd
  elements in document order, and then to process that list by iterating only
  the bookmarkStart elements and yielding only those that have a unique name
  and have a matching end _subsequent_ to the start.
  
  Something roughly like this perhaps:

  ```python
  class BookmarkFinder(object):
  
      def __init__(self, story):
          self._story = story
  
      @classmethod
      def start_end_pairs(cls, story):
          """Return list of (bookmarkStart, bookmarkEnd) pairs for *story*.
  
          *story* is a document element, later maybe header, footer, etc.
          """
          return list(cls(story)._iter_start_end_pairs())
  
      def _iter_start_end_pairs(self):
          for idx, bookmarkStart in self.iter_starts():
              # ---skip open pairs---
              bookmarkEnd = self._matching_end(bookmarkStart, idx)
              if bookmarkEnd is None:
                  continue
              # ---skip duplicate names---
              if not self._add_to_names_so_far(bookmarkStart.name):
                  continue
              yield (bookmarkStart, bookmarkEnd)
  
      @lazyproperty
      def _all_starts_and_ends(self):
          return story.xpath(...something clever...)
  
      def _iter_starts(self):
          """Generate (idx, bookmarkStart) elements in story.
  
          The *idx* value indicates the location of the bookmarkStart element
          among all the bookmarkStart and bookmarkEnd elements in the story.
          """
          for idx, element in enumerate(self._all_starts_and_ends):
              if element.tag = qn('w:bookmarkStart'):
                  yield idx, element
  
      def _matching_end(self, bookmarkStart, idx):
          for element in self._all_starts_and_ends[idx + 1:]:
              # ---skip bookmark starts---
              if element.tag = qn('w:bookmarkStart'):
                  continue
              bookmarkEnd = element
              if bookmarkEnd.id == bookmarkStart.id:
                  return bookmarkEnd
          return None
  
      def _add_to_names_so_far(self, name):
          """Return True if name was added, False if name already present."""
          names_so_far = self._names_so_far
          if name in names_so_far:
              return False
          names_so_far.add(name)
          return True
  
      @lazyproperty
      def _names_so_far(self):
          return set()
  ```

This would be called as `BookmarkFinder.start_end_pairs(document_element)` on each sequence method (len, etc.) and then used either for it's length, or as the raw material to create one or more `Bookmark` proxy objects.

We're always interested in contributions, and it's very wise to do as you've done to reach out before investing time in a pull request.

Contributing is a bit challenging for most folks, in many cases because they are unaccustomed to the Test-driven approach we use here. No code, no matter how clever or useful, is merged without the tests to support it. And in general, a successful contribution requires an analysis page such as those you can find in the documentation here:

So the first question would be whether you were willing to develop those items in conjunction with your code. Most folks who have tried it so far have found it a rewarding learning experience, but many folks  prefer to work without supporting tests.

I am very reticent to revive the learner/coach switcher - because I think it promotes the conflation of 'role' and 'membership'. Being a member of a collection (facility, class, group) is very different from having a role with respect to that class, the only exception here is facility, where everyone with a role wrt to the facility is also a member of the facility.

In the case of classes and groups, there are many cases where I think it seems odd to have to include the person with a role wrt to that collection in the collection before assigning them a role, teacher with class, peer coach for a group.

Hi @kat-co I am sorry for your bad experience with charm build.  I also get this same error message when issuing the same command.  The command you were looking for is: `charm build --help`

The reason you are seeing this error is because the command you issued is looking for a layer named "help".  The build command should not stack trace when the name is not found and should provide a proper error message with a usage.

```sh
}
Traceback (most recent call last):
```

I looked at making micropython import `autobahn.asyncio.wamp.ApplicationSession`, it would require adding/faking many dependencies in MicroPython (mainly from standard CPython library).

I am not sure whether it makes sense to add support for MicroPython in Autobahn core, or if creating an optimised autobahn-micropython would make more sense :
* there are a lot of dependencies that make sense in the CPython implementation but little in MicroPython (eg: zlib, six, ...).
* size can be an issue: the autobahn-python library is 1.3 MB (v0.16.0) and MicroPython itself is in the 524 kB range. Some boards only have 1 MB of flash.
* many class abstractions make readable code, but are very slow on embedded devices

Pulled this down and got it running. This is a workable foundation for forecasting problems. However, the current version is not usable for classification problems.

Some thoughts:

1) `extract_features` drops a row from the input `x`. 
In the notebook, for example: x starts out with shape `(200,)`.
After `make_forecasting_frame` and `extract_features` it has shape `(199, 275)`.
Rather than dropping the rows, it should maintain the original indices and set empty values as a result of lagged data frames  to `NaN` like `.rolling` in pandas. Otherwise, it is challenging to work around for most machine learning problems where `X` is your data and `y` is your ground truth/target. Assume you are working from a dataset with 400 rows and `X` are your features, `y` is your ground truth for classification. If your `X` ends up with 399 rows after feature extraction and your `y` is at 400, this is a problem. 

2) It seems like it would be desirable if `make_forecasting_frame` + `extract_features` would generate the best features for `X` with respect to `y`, as opposed to assuming that its a forecasting problem. Of course, in a forecasting/regression problem, the best features are usually those that coincide with accurately predicting the next window of the time-series, so you don't really need a separate `y`. In classification, obviously you need a separate `y` target that your model is trying to predict. One way to achieve this with `tsfresh` would be: add a forecasting flag that if set, uses `n` number of following time-series rows to generate a `y`. If forecasting is 0, then tsfresh expects to receive an explicit `y` data frame or array. This seems like it would be the most `sklearn`-like approach, and would be flexible to both forecasting and classification problems. Probably in this case it should be called `make_rolling_frame` or `make_lag_frame` as to be general to either forecasting or classification.

I agree that less magic is better, but *some* magic is useful.

The treatment of includes vs libs is inconsistent.  conda-build defines LIBRARY_PATH=$PREFIX/lib, so why wouldn't it do the same for includes?

The answer is that is does *try* - it defines INCLUDE_PATH=$PREFIX/include, but that isn't honored by gcc (nor by clang I think).

This seems confusing.  Why define INCLUDE_PATH if it has no effect?  I would argue that either all four (INCLUDE_PATH, C_INCLUDE_PATH, CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH, LIBRARY_PATH) should be defined, or none.

I'm not going to lose sleep over it, but it does seem like a trap of confusion and bugs.  Thanks

Python 2 is basically incapable of handling unicode properly. What you've run into here is exactly that. In this case, if any character in your `PATH` env. var contains anything that is not ASCII then it throws this exception.

All I can suggest is that you either remove all non-ASCII from `PATH` or switch to Python 3 instead.

Hi @janschulz,

We hear and understand your frustration. The 4.1.x implementation of channel priority is only the first step. We **will** continue to both refine and add to these capabilities. More intelligent "stickiness" of packages to their original channels is part of the plan. 

There is also much more coming with the aim of improving all of conda features, stability, and communication. Both process improvements along with code improvements. 

For example, conda 4.2 will be released initially under a new *conda canary* program (which I hope you'll opt in to participating in).

Conda definitely has a huge backlog of open issues still, but in the last couple months we've dramatically improved on our responsiveness to open pull requests. Especially for things like better documentation both in command-line help and online documentation, contributed pull requests are very much appreciated. 

As the conda community and ecosystem grows (e.g. conda-forge awesomeness, Intel distribution, etc), we're working hard to make sure users get what they need, want, and expect. We know we're not done yet and have further to go.

Definitely appreciate your feedback and input.

fixed with #3326

I think there is a conda issue here (https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2062). `conda update --all` installs the `mkl` feature metapackage, even if you have `nomkl` installed (which causes additional `conda update --all` calls to give Unsatisfiable Package Specifications errors). I didn't check the metadata so I can't say if the issue is that you set up the features for the mkl packages incorrectly or that conda's solver is doing the wrong thing with features. Given what I know about the solver, though, I'm guessing it's the latter. And even if the metadata is wrong, `conda update --all` ought to be idempotent, and it definitely shouldn't put the environment in an unsatisfiable state.
It seems the the package might not have been installed with conda, but maybe with pip?  Without more information, there's not really any way to know.  Glad you got the issue resolved.

> Would conda be supporting using the activate.bat script in the future?

We want to get rid of the `activate` "executable", because it has name collisions with something like virtualenv's `activate`.  But I agree that we have an unsolved issue here before we can fully end up deprecating it.

> but the rest of conda.bat is not run. Isn't that a problem?

The answer is, it depends.  For now, using `conda.exe` instead of `conda` is no different than what we had in 4.3.  Besides adding real `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` commands, the only other thing `conda.bat` does is call "reactivate" for `install`, `update`, and `remove`.  But conda 4.3 never did "reactivate", so at least you're no worse off using `conda.exe`.

[libsolv](https://github.com/openSUSE/libsolv) (possibly with [hawkey](https://github.com/rpm-software-management/hawkey) for the Python API)? If you set the parameter in a condarc or config file, you set it as, e.g.

    subdir: osx-32

If you want to use it as an environment variable, you could do something like

    CONDA_SUBDIR=osx-32 conda create -n py34 python=3.4

The `_subdir` in the code let's me make a `context.subdir` property where I can do more logic to customize the default value if `subdir` isn't set via configuration anywhere.

Technically you could also set `_subdir: osx-32` or `CONDA__SUBDIR=osx-32` I guess, but the alias part allows you to use subdir in the more natural way.

This is what I  use in our code collection's setup.py (see https://github.com/CGATOxford/cgat/blob/master/setup.py)

'''(python)
pyx_files = glob.glob("scripts/*.pyx")
script_extensions = []
pysam_dirname = os.path.dirname(pysam.__file__)
include_dirs = [numpy.get_include()] + pysam.get_include()

if IS_OSX:
    # linking against bundles does no work (and apparently is not needed)
    # within OS X
    extra_link_args = []
else:
    extra_link_args = [os.path.join(pysam_dirname, x) for x in (
        'libchtslib.so',
        'TabProxies.so',
        'cfaidx.so',
        'csamfile.so',
        'cvcf.so',
        'ctabix.so')]

for pyx_file in pyx_files:
    script_name = os.path.basename(pyx_file)
    script_prefix = script_name[:-4]
    script_extensions.append(
        Extension("CGAT.%s" % (script_prefix),
                  sources=[pyx_file],
                  extra_link_args=extra_link_args,
                  include_dirs=include_dirs,
                  define_macros=pysam.get_defines())
    )
'''
Note that it seems not be necessary to use pysam.get_libraries()

@joshrsmith Yes it is in theory possible to use VUnit as a test runner without the VUnit library. It is not something that we have support for using the Public API. We cache the account info of a user upon `az login`. 
The problem is that interactive looks into a different folder for this cached information and thus would require another login from interactive mode to get this information. 
Unfortunately, a second login is not possible in cloudshell. A workaround until our next release would be to copy over the contents of ".azure" into ".azure-shell".
https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/pull/5442 will make interactive mode look at the .azure for this info.

This is happening because the code that pulls in the docstring help text is preserving the line break.

The original text looks like:
```
        Break the lease, if the container has an active lease. Once a lease is
        broken, it cannot be renewed. Any authorized request can break the lease;
        the request is not required to specify a matching lease ID. When a lease
        is broken, the lease break period is allowed to elapse, during which time
        no lease operation except break and release can be performed on the container.
        When a lease is successfully broken, the response indicates the interval
        in seconds until a new lease can be acquired. 
```
Since the first sentence is promoted to the short description, the first line gets shortened. If the library handles the text wrapping, we should be able to address this by stripping out the line breaks.

I'm certainly fine with placing the module elsewhere in the CLI. I just wasn't sure where a good home would be.

This module is indeed consumed by VSCode extension. The point of hosting it in the CLI is that we can update it should any of its assumptions break and release that together with the breaking changes as part of the CLI. With such an approach the VSCode extension not (or less often) have to update in anticipation of breaking changes in the CLI. The VSCode extension will also not (or less often) have to add version detection to handle different versions of the CLI.

@derekbekoe would be best able to answer that, but in general, `azure-cli (2.0.6)` is the version of the `azure-cli` PIP package, and the `0.2.9-1` is the "package version" of the bundled apt-get package.

pre-commit deals only in passing filenames to hooks.  By staged contents, it means the contents of the file that would exist if the commit would succeed (and not unstaged changes).

Exclude takes a regex.  Simply combine the two things you want to exclude with a |:

```yaml 
    exclude: '^(path/1|other/path2)$'
```

   Implementation above would allow to both have the trace config global for the session as well as adding them dynamically with `async with session.trace_configs`. 


   For the sake of comlicity I should tell what `TaskLocals` is. It's the same concept as `contextvars` which will be introduced in python 3.7 (https://docs.python.org/dev/library/contextvars.html#module-contextvars). The implementation I borrowed from `peewee_async` library. It looks as follows:
   ```
   class TaskLocals:
       """Simple `dict` wrapper to get and set values on per `asyncio`
    task basis.

    The idea is similar to thread-local data, but actually *much* simpler.
    It's no more than a "sugar" class. Use `get()` and `set()` method like
    you would to for `dict` but values will be get and set in the context
    of currently running `asyncio` task.

    When task is done, all saved values is removed from stored data.
    """
       def __init__(self, loop):
           self.loop = loop
           self.data = {}

       def get(self, key, *val):
           """Get value stored for current running task. Optionally
        you may provide the default value. Raises `KeyError` when
        can't get the value and no default one is provided.
        """
           data = self.get_data()
           if data is not None:
               return data.get(key, *val)
           elif len(val):
               return val[0]
           else:
               raise KeyError(key)

       def set(self, key, val):
           """Set value stored for current running task.
        """
           data = self.get_data(True)
           if data is not None:
               data[key] = val
           else:
               raise RuntimeError("No task is currently running")

       def get_data(self, create=False):
           """Get dict stored for current running task. Return `None`
        or an empty dict if no data was found depending on the
        `create` argument value.

        :param create: if argument is `True`, create empty dict
                       for task, default: `False`
        """
           task = asyncio.Task.current_task(loop=self.loop)
           if task:
               task_id = id(task)
               if create and not task_id in self.data:
                   self.data[task_id] = {}
                   task.add_done_callback(self.del_data)
               return self.data.get(task_id)

       def del_data(self, task):
           """Delete data for task from stored data dict.
        """
           del self.data[id(task)]
   ```

For reference, Donald Stufft mentioned some problems with entry points in a thread on distutils-sig. I don't think it should necessarily stop us using stuff (especially because I'm not sure there's any alternative without similar issues), but it's worth being aware of the drawbacks:

> One that I was helping someone debug just the other day is that they’re super non-debuggable and the behavior when you have two things providing the same entry point is basically ???? (If I remember correctly, the behavior is that the first thing found is the one that “wins”, which means the ordering of sys.path and the names of the projects supply it is what determines it). This got exposed to the end user that they installed something that they thought was going to add support for something, but which silently did nothing because two different project happened to pick the same name for their entry point (not the group, it was two things providing plugins for the same system).
>
> Of course there is the perennial entrypoints are super slow, which is partially the fault of pkg_resources does a bunch of import time logic, but also because scanning sys.path for all installed stuff is just slow.
>
> They’re also somewhat fragile since they rely on the packaging metadata system at runtime, and a number of tools exclude that information (often times things that deploy stuff as a tarball/zipfile) which causes regular issues to be opened up for these projects when they get used in those environments.
>
> Those are the ones I remember because they come up regularly (and people regularly come to me with issues with any project related to packaging in any way even for non packaging related features in those projects). I’m pretty sure there were more of them that I’ve encountered and seen projects encounter, but I can’t remember them to be sure.

I have some plans to do caching to mitigate the slowness issue (that's partly what the distutils-sig thread is about) - but that's inevitably going to lead to a new kind of problem, with caches getting out of date. We'll have to wait for the implementation to know how big a problem that will be in practice.

The tricky thing is deciding how to treat a cell being queued multiple times. There are multiple right answers, depending on user assumptions. Options include:

1. Queue the cell multiple times, but actually execute it when the kernel is available (i.e., that's when the text is 'frozen' in the execution call)
2. Move a cell to the back of the queue each time it is executed
3. Let the cell "execute" and take the text at that point in time. If the cell is modified and re-executed, the new execution text is entered in the queue also, and doesn't replace earlier executions.

I think (3) is the least surprising, but I can also see users thinking that they can edit a cell's contents before it is actually executed and getting those edits in under the wire.

I'd like to try and explain what is going on with the encoding issues.

On a working build, we are able to easily change the locale with environment variables and see the following.

    RUN python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.getfilesystemencoding())"
    utf-8

However, on Circle CI we are running into this bug ( https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/langpack-locales/+bug/931717 ). As a result, we see this.

    RUN python3 -c "import sys; print(sys.getfilesystemencoding())"
    ascii

There is a workaround proposed here ( https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/apparmor/+bug/969299/comments/6 ). Though, I don't believe we can take advantage of this. When talking with someone from Circle CI, they stated that the best thing to do would be to have an image with the locale settings workaround done. I went ahead and did that and put it in the image here ( https://github.com/jakirkham/docker_ubuntu_14_04_locale_fix ). This fixes the problem and makes the patch unnecessary.

With these changes the build will pass. ( https://circleci.com/gh/jakirkham/notebook/75 )

> Re `_initial`: If the user needs the original data, they can always just add a property dynamically or subclass.

Yeah, I think you're totally right.

Another thing that's a big question mark to me is `Model.__optionsclass__`. I guess one could imagine it being mildly useful... if you ignore the fact that it's not even inherited:

```python
class A(Model):
    __optionsclass__ = MyOptions


class B(A):
    class Options:
        ... # These won't use the MyOptions class.
```

If there's enthusiasm to improve on this, we could do a soft deprecation of `__optionsclass__` along with the introduction of a new mechanism that gets looked up on the superclasses as well.

But I wonder if this whole feature makes much sense. If it's only to create static option templates for easy reuse across an application, then that could be accomplished with a more lightweight system:

```python
class MyStandardOptions:
    namespace = 'foo'


class X(Model):
    Options = BaseOptions   # This is already possible.


class Y(Model):
    class Options(MyStandardOptions):  # This would be easy to implement.
        export_order = True
```

I think that the right answer is between @simonchx and @rht. 

The 9-grid form will place the blue circle where all the green circles are. Our comparison point is the black circle.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31418520/32850221-8f7e211e-c9ff-11e7-808e-ca1e74febd57.png)

The squares are what happens from the `pos_1 = (pos_1 - self.center) % self.size` commands. 

So in many cases the mod happens to be right, but in the one in the image it differs from the actual shortest distance to the 9-grid version. 

The following code should work:

```python
dx = np.abs(pos_1[0] - pos_2[0])
dy = np.abs(pos_1[1] - pos_2[1])
if torus:
    dx = min(dx, size[0] - dx)
    dy = min(dy, size[1] - dy)
return np.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy)
```
In a torus, the two possible x and y distances sum to the width and height, respectively. Picking the smallest of those gets the shortest distance. 

The above code can then be used to get the torus-adjusted heading (dx, dy), and `get_distance` would only need to call for the heading and do the final line.

Hi all,

The `dotnetcore-windowshosting` Chocolatey package is designed to install the ANCM (and only the ANCM).

1. If the user requests Chocolatey to install the `dotnetcore-windowshosting` package, the expected result after the installation is that the ANCM gets installed on the machine. That is the reason for the IIS/IIS Express precondition check in the package installation script: because the installation bundle skips installing the ANCM if neither of them is installed, it would not make sense for the `dotnetcore-windowshosting` package to report successful installation if nothing is actually installed on the system.

2. The runtime and the package store have their own packages (`dotnetcore-runtime.install` and `aspnetcore-runtimepackagestore`). Required dependencies are modelled using package dependency (the `dotnetcore-windowshosting` package depends upon `aspnetcore-runtimepackagestore`) and they are resolved by the package manager. Installation of optional dependencies (the runtime) is left to the discretion of the user*.

*At least, this is how things worked in 1.0 and 1.1. I can see that the 2.0 hosting bundle does not respect the OPT_INSTALL_LTS_REDIST and OPT_INSTALL_FTS_REDIST flags anymore and installs the 2.0 runtime anyway. Is this a bug or intentional? (If the latter, then https://github.com/aspnet/AspNetCoreModule/blob/dev/README.md should probably be updated.)

From packaging standpoint, it would be most convenient if you distributed an installer containing the ANCM only.

@swaletzko Probably what is happening is that the .NET Core library is targeting a higher version of .NET Standard than works with the version of .NET Framework you are targeting.  Target a lower version of .NET Standard with the .NET Core library and your .NET Framework projects should be able to reference it.

See the ".NET Platforms Support" table in the [.NET Standard Library documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/articles/standard/library) to determine what versions of .NET Standard can be used with what version of .NET Framework.  The table might be confusing, but basically if you are targeting .NET 4.5 then you can reference libraries targeting .NET Standard 1.1 or lower.  If you target .NET 4.6 then you can reference .NET Standard 1.3 or lower.

The change wasn't in mArchiveList but in the underlying web service.  The older version of the service used a spatial index for finding images based on point locations (the center of the images).  To find all the images overlapping a region, the region was padded by the diagonal size of a 2MASS image.  This is equivalent to having the images be circular (bounding circle) regions.

Some months ago, we updated the web service to use an R-Tree index (based on a new Montage module).  The images could then be real polygon regions on the sky.  However, we forgot to take the padding out of the search region calculation, so we have been getting a slightly-too-large return.  Since in most cases we use a tool like mSubset to trim the list down exactly,
the larger list doesn't have too much an effect but it could cause some people to download images they don't need.

The padding has now been removed and in this case the service reduces the count even further since a couple of those eight matched with the bounding circle but not an exact polygon check.

@littleday I'm not entirely sure why this would be different between the python shell and IPython or Django, but I'm guessing those other shells are on different machines, and that the first machine is set to Pacific time.

The reason for this is because "PDT" and other 3-letter acronyms are not sufficient to uniquely specify a time zone, as many or most of them are not unique (and we don't have a great way of maintaining a mapping, anyway). These are really only useful *in context*, and so you have to supply the context for the parser. One of the places the parser looks for context is in the time zone abbreviations your machine exposes through `time.tzname`, and if it finds the abbreviation there, it says, "Oh, this must be in your local time" and attaches `tzlocal`. Otherwise if it *looks* like a time zone abbreviation and doesn't find it anywhere, it just skips over it.

To make it work consistently on any machine, do this:

```python
from dateutil import tz
from dateutil.parser import parse
PT = tz.gettz('US/Pacific')
tzinfos = {'PST': PT, 'PDT': PT}
dt = parse("2017/10/03 09:59:52 PDT", tzinfos=tzinfos)
```

Similarly if you want to add other abbrebiations, add them in the `tzinfos` dictionary. Alternatively, you can write a function that takes `tzname` abbreviations and returns tzinfo objects.

Hope this is helpful!

You must run your daemon with a UTF-8 locale. If you run it with non-UTF-8 locale it will assume that all its clients have same non-UTF-8 locale and thus disable fancy characters. Separators are correct for this case.

This should also be fixed by `--restart` from a UTF-8 locale, not `--kill`.

Thanks Alex!

**2D attributes**: Yes, this makes sense and would be easy to implement.

**Graphs**: In Loom, graphs are stored as (row,  col, weight) arrays, equivalent to the coo format from scipy.sparse. This supports arbitrary weighted, directed and undirected graphs (even multigraphs, I suppose). `MKNN` is a mutual KNN, i.e. a KNN with non-mutual edges removed. It doesn't have a fixed K any longer of course.

**Unstructured data**: can you explain in more detail what you allow as unstructured, and how that is written and read to the HDF5 format?

I implemented modification timestamps as follows:

#### In the loom file itself

The HDF5 attribute `last_modified` is set to an ISO8601 timestamp in the UTC timezone in the compact format (e.g. `20180124T100436.901000Z`).

The `last_modified` HDF5 attribute is set on:

```
/ (the root of the file)
/matrix
/layers/{name}
/row_edges
/row_edges/{name}
/col_edges
/col_edges/{name}
/row_attrs
/row_attrs/{name}
/col_attrs
/col_attrs/{name}
```

The modification timestamp at any level indicates the most recent modification time for any item below it in the HDF5 hierarchy.

#### In loompy

`ds.last_modified()`: Modification timestamp for whole file. Will timestamp the file if it doesn't have a timestamp already.

`ds.layers.last_modified()`: Timestamp for layers
`ds.layers.last_modified(name)`: Timestamp for specific layer

`ds.col_attrs.last_modified()`: Timestamp for column attributes
`ds.col_attrs.last_modified(name)`: Timestamp for specific column attribute

And so on for column attrs and graphs.

Finally, you can get a changeset relative to a given timestamp, like so:

`ds.get_changes_since(timestamp)`: returns a dictionary of layers, attributes and graphs that have been modified since the given timestamp. For example:

```
with loompy.connect("/Users/sten/build_20171205_bak/L5_All.loom") as ds:
    print(ds.get_changes_since("20180124T100436.901000Z"))
```

I don't know about Java, but in .NET this doesn't work because the context may be unreachable before all objects containing references to it are destroyed, e.g., think of a large set of ASTs, each of which has a reference to the Context, but the whole set of them has just become unreachable. Frequently the GC will now try to finalize the Context, and only after that it will handle the ASTs. It will crash because the Context, and the underlying native Z3_Context, are not there anymore. This is why we count the number of ASTs and if there is an attempt to finalize the Context while the counter is still greater than zero, then the Context is put back onto the finalization queue. 

Since we can't requeue objects onto the finalization queue, I think the only real solution would be to implement our own finalization queue (one way or another), because we can't teach the GC that there are also dependencies between the underlying native objects.

Contributions are very welcome. 

The biggest consideration for contributors is whether the features are stable or get maintained and responsiveness with respect to bug fixes.
 
Functionality that bit-rots gets cut. There is limited bandwidth to deal with stale features.
API features are occasionally added. It helps when there is a systematic and straight-forward way to expose these features for new interfaces.

There are some APIs to Z3 that are not part of this repository. E.g., there is/were some Haskell bindings. 
There is also some interest in usable JavaScript bindings (see #1298).

Regarding the Java idioms, they are similar to the .Net conventions. The example you list above resembles the Python bindings API, which is considerably more user friendly for scripting. Some
of these features can be carried over to the Java bindings based on popular demand, but overall
there are no plans to change the overall way the bindings are exposed. An application that needs something efficient should bypass the Java/.Net bindings that creates objects for every expression.
Unfortunately, it is too error prone (every expression becomes a ulong or IntPtr) to use such bindings so it is not something that is worth exposing for general consumption.

suppose I disable the code that bails out on unbounded objectives during lex, then I get funny answers because later objectives could recycle some of the same values. Take for instance:

    (set-option :produce-models true)
    (declare-fun x () Int)
    (declare-fun y () Int)
    (assert (< x 4))
    (assert (< y 4))
    (minimize x)
    (maximize y)
    (maximize x)
    (check-sat)

With the modified lex I get:
    sat
    (objectives
     (x  (interval (* (- 1) oo) (- 2)))
     (y 3)
     (x (- 2))
   )

Now, the value of x got set to -2 because this maximized the third objective. But it acted contrary to the first objective which is the lexicographically most significant. You could argue that it is wrong to skip the part that constrains the first objective in case it is unbounded. It should be "infinitely small" no matter what. So what does it mean to add this constraint? In one sense it means "whatever value x has it should not be a finite number". But this means that any assignment to x is infeasible, e.g., assert false.
So this behavior could be argued as even more counter-intuitive.

Yes, that would be possible, but it would be inconsistent with the C++ and Python APIs, and the Java API as well (Java doesn't support overloading). Note that & and && are not the same in C++ (or any other language), specifically, if their result is used in an if-condition, the result of & will silently be cast to an int and compared to zero, but in Z3 there are no silent casts. If things were the other way around (&& overloadable, not &), we could think about adding just the Boolean case (not everybody needs BitVectors, but everybody needs Booleans), but that isn't the case. So after re-evaluating the situation, I'm still opposed to adding this.

Sorry, @NikolajBjorner @cheshire, I don't agree with that. There are enough applications that use many contexts in loops for us to consider them and there will soon be many more. For instance, all verifiers that try to parallelize queries create lots of temporary context objects. Also, for existing applications we can't force authors to refactor their code, which may well be significant effort for some of them.

The only real problem with finalization is that Java doesn't provide a way to re-queue an object onto the finalization queue once its finalizer runs. The solution to that problem is to implement our own finalization queue, which we already do for all non-context objects anyway. That's significant, but not huge, effort and as long as I'm not missing something, that way we should be able to support both mechanisms.

Hi,

I don't know if you ever investigated this problem. I have found one issue that can help fix this problem. Nonetheless, in one of my research project (short paper at ASE 2017 on symbolic pointers), I am still seeing issues related to wrong results from claripy solvers. However, I am not able to produce a small testcase since the error shows up in a very long and complex experiment. 

Anyway, one reason for this issue could be due to one wrong assumption made in `ModelCacheMixin`. Suppose that we ask for `n` solutions for a symbolic expression to claripy, where `n > MODEL_LIMIT` (i.e., we ask for more solutions than the maximum number of models that are allowed to be cached). Assuming that the cache is initially empty, `batch_eval` finds 0 cached models and thus calls the [`batch_eval` function](https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/frontend_mixins/model_cache_mixin.py#L254) of the super class.  Solutions are added to the cache by [`_model_hook`](https://github.com/angr/claripy/blob/master/claripy/frontend_mixins/model_cache_mixin.py#L202). However, only `MODEL_LIMIT` models are actually inserted in the cache. Before returning the solutions, `batch_eval` checks whether all the possible models have been actually found:
 ```
        if len(extra_constraints) == 0 and len(results) < n:
            self._eval_exhausted.update(e.cache_key for e in asts)
```
If this is the case, then `e` is marked as exhausted. However, this is assuming that all the models have been stored in the cache. Indeed, if another call to  `batch_eval` is done on the same expression, then the exhausted flag is checked and only the cached solutions are returned:
```
        if len(results) == n or (len(asts) == 1 and asts[0].cache_key in self._eval_exhausted):
            return results
```
Possible fix: the exhausted flag should be an integer. This should be set to the maximum number of solutions that have been found by the solver when exhausting the solution space. If the cache does not return this number of solutions then the exhausted flag is not valid.

The tuple/list thing is a problem. We can just coerce it into a list and then do the concat as a list.

The latter thing is complicated. The thing you've suggested is confusing because `self._size` doesn't exist, but really it's kind of a moot point because that check is _supposed_ to be an abort condition. If you're really trying to store more than 16MB in a single go, something is _very_ wrong and you should debug the cause instead of trying to make it just work.

Invalidating the cache is not trivial:
- a cache entry is indexed by `(addr, insn_bytes, max_size, num_inst, thumb, opt_level)`
- dirty addresses due to a IRSB are tracked by `state.scratch.dirty_addrs`
- to invalidate a cache entry as soon as some addresses are marked as dirty, we have to check if the entry is inside the cache... but to generate the cache key, we also need to know `(insn_bytes, max_size, num_inst, thumb, opt_level)` used in the past (trying all the possible combinations is not the best approach). However, if we are dealing with self-modifying code, we can assume that `opt_level=0`. I don't think we can make other assumptions on `(max_size, num_inst, thumb)` (they could be changed during a project lifetime)
- another approach requires to invalidate an entry when the lifter looks for it (hence `(insn_bytes, max_size, num_inst, thumb, opt_level)` are known) but this requires to keep track of dirty addresses across different IRSB. I don't like this strategy.

Hey, sorry for taking so long to get back to you! Here is some advice:

- Use the `CGC_ZERO_FILL_UNCONSTRAINED_MEMORY` state option. This will make unconstrained memory accesses return zero instead of a symbolic value.
- If possible, use the `STRICT_PAGE_ACCESS` option. This will cause angr to strictly enforce page mapping and permissions, and raise a `SimSegfaultError` whenever there's an action that would cause a segfault in a real program - usually this indicates either a bug in angr's environment model or a bug in the guest program.
- Disable the `LAZY_SOLVES` state option.
- Use the `full_init_state` instead of the `entry_state`. The entry state is the default for constructing paths and path groups if no state is provided, so you should specify this manually if you were using those.
- Do not disable simprocedures - they are your best friend when it comes to minimizing interaction with messy linux internals that we don't implement correctly.

Unfortunately, we don't have an environment model that can execute complex programs concretely. Linux is really complicated, and even though I've personally spent a ton of time on what I call "accuracy work" (read more about this [here](https://github.com/angr/angr/issues/196#issuecomment-242264186)), there is still a long way to go :(

> you seem to know quite a bit about his

Being a core libpcap developer for many years helps. :-)

> is there a feature request here?

I would say "yes".

I would, on UN\*Xes, provide an API that uses `pcap_get_selectable_fd()` if available and `pcap_fileno()` otherwise.  Whether it should fail or return `fileno(pcap_file(pcap))` if `pcap_file(pcap)` is non-null is another matter - `select()`/`poll()` on a plain file should always return TRUE, but that means there's no point in doing a `select()` or `poll()` on it unless the program logic makes it harder *not* to do so.

I would, on Windows, *not* provide that API, because there isn't a file descriptor that can be provided that would be usable with WinSock's `select()` call, and would, instead, provide an API that uses `pcap_getevent()`, which returns a `HANDLE` that can be used with `WaitForSingleObject()`, `WaitForSingleObjectEx()`, `WaitForMultipleObjects()`, `WaitForMultipleObjectsEx()`, `MsgWaitForMultipleObjects()`, and `MsgWaitForMultipleObjectsEx()`.

Thank your @scottinet for the feedback !

> Offline-first client and Decentralised discovery?

The offline-first client allows application developers to implement an application that works offline. The developers would just create/update/delete records, and the library takes care of synchronizing the local database with the remote server. See [kinto.js](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto.js) or [PouchDB](http://pouchdb.com/)

Decentralized discovery is the ability for the application to store the data at a specific location automatically. Most likely that depends on the authenticated user. When I use an application and say that I am mathieu@mydomain.com then the application knows that my Kinto instance is hosted at https://kinto.framasoft.net. See [remotestorage](https://remotestorage.io/) for example.

> Conflict resolution

Do you have any details to provide ? This is a very wide topic and if you do something in particular I think it would be great to mention it in our docs :) 
I realize that *Kinto* does not really perform any automatic conflict resolution. It's more about *concurrency control*, using `Etag`.

> Bulk operations 

Noted. From what I read, Kuzzle does only bulk create (import) though.

> Validation, through pipe plugins

Noted ! 
I think we should probably distinguish what is available via code, and via HTTP API. In for example Kinto you defined a JSON Schema on the collection via the HTTP API, and created/updated records will be validated for this collection.

> Fine-grained permissions

Great!
I read the documentation (and almost the whole pull-request) and from what I understood (may be wrong), the permissions are set via configuration (or code), but not via HTTP API.

> Pluggable authentication

Noted :)

There are a few possibilities for API on the coefficients.

**Here is one possibility for a coefficient dictionary:**
``fswt``/``ifswt`` are most closely related in function to ``wavedecn``/``waverecn``.  Let's assume as an example case a full decomposition on an array of dimension 256 x 8.  Here ``wavedecn`` would perform at most 3 levels for a Haar wavelet and each level would correspond to a coefficient dictionary with entries 'ad', 'da', 'dd'.  
However for ``fswt``, the number of levels would be different along each axis: (8, 3).  The transform is first 8 levels of decomposition along axis 0 followed by 3 levels along axis 1 for a total of 8x3=24 sets of coefficients.  It is not possible to cleanly separate these into a list corresponding to "levels" as in ``wavedecn`` anymore.  If we were to use a dictionary, I think the keys would have to be something like a tuple equal in length to the number of axes where (0, 0) would be the approximation coefficients and (1, 3) for example would correspond to one level of decomposition along axis 0 and 3 levels along axis 1.

Hi @zenving 

First, regarding the functionality of the `colorizer`:
1. By default, it will convert any 16-bit depth image to an RGB image using the JET color scheme. 
2. You can enable / disable histogram equalization using `RS2_OPTION_HISTOGRAM_EQUALIZATION_ENABLED` option. Generally speaking, histogram equalization produce images with more details, but it can also exaggerate some of the noise if the dynamic range is low. 
3. When histogram equalization is disabled, the color map will be scaled from `RS2_OPTION_MIN_DISTANCE` to `RS2_OPTION_MAX_DISTANCE` in meters.
4. We implemented several color maps you can use out of the box by settings the `RS2_OPTION_COLOR_SCHEME` option. You can get the total number of color maps using `get_option_range` method.

Now regarding the use of the `colorizer` in code: We often use `colorizer` to shape depth data into something the user can more easily process. For consistency and clarity we use the `colorizer` even when better methods exist (like with the Unity wrapper, where it makes much more sense to implement this conversion in a shader, or in OpenCV examples, where you can achieve a similar effect with couple of OpenCV calls). 

I somewhat regret using the `colorizer` in the Grab-Cut example. Sure, it works, and we saw an opportunity to showcase RealSense API, but in context of OpenCV example it would make more sense to use standard OpenCV tools.

For returning contributors 10% or higher: I wouldn't suggest having a variable number of features (i.e, it's fine if up-front the Featurizer allows the user to choose the top N options and always returns N options, but not for it to return <N or >N options in that case).

Basically two reasons:

* It might mess up some technical things like the featurize_dataframe() method which featurizes every row and adds it the dataframe. If different rows have different numbers of features returned, it could cause a problem. These technical things could be fixed (if they are a problem in the first place) with some effort though.
* We will need each row to contain the same number of features for scikit-learn at the end. We can certainly fill any missing values with NaN to make it work out if different rows have different numbers of features. However, It is less desirable to have (say) the 4th feature be "contribution of 4th highest orbital if it's >10% and float.NaN otherwise" rather than just "contribution of 4th highest orbital" which is a more continuous measure. Thus, since you need the same number of features in the end, it's good to just have it be that way since the beginning and use a smoother / more consistent definition of that feature.

Thus I'd suggest just letting the user choose the number N in the top N features regardless of any hard cutoff like 10%.

For Fermi smearing, certainly implement how you'd like, but note that hard cutoffs can lead to sharper discontinuities in feature values as a function of that cutoff.

Can't use original hash func because: "Python's hash function is designed for speed, and maps into a 64-bit space. Due to the birthday paradox, this means you'll likely get a collision at about 5 billion entries (probably way earlier, since the hash function is not cryptographical). Also, the precise definition of hash is up to the Python implementation, and may be architecture- or even machine-specific. Don't use it you want the same result on multiple machines."  -- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/7646520/is-this-an-appropriate-use-of-pythons-built-in-hash-function

- It allowed us to introduce the performance and reliability benefits of mirror.bazel.build.
- It grants corporations and nations the flexibility to tune their networks to opt-in or opt-out of trusting service providers.
- It helps ensure old revisions always work.
- It ensures builds don't break when webmasters copy libraries like zlib into a different folder.
- It reduces the burden our CI systems place on service providers such as GitHub.
- It helps facilitate license compliance auditing
- It helps facilitate security auditing

The mirror.bazel.build domain is linked to a multiregional GCS bucket. The Bazel team was extremely generous in owning the burden of safely housing these public artifacts indefinitely, in service to users, to promote the health of its ecosystem.

There are even compilers that place packed decorations before *and* after the declaration, such as [CodeWarrior](http://www.nxp.com/files/soft_dev_tools/doc/white_paper/CWPORTINGWP.pdf).  I noted that the existing `toolchain.h` file in this PR seems to include CodeWarrior support, but the packed defines are empty.  Have we tested all of the included compilers?  We should only include compiler support for those we have tested against.

You're not really using pyfftw properly. The builders return an FFTW object, they don't actually do the FFT - you have to call the returned FFTW object for that. Moreover, you don't want to be doing the building _inside_ your function - best is to use a class that maintains knowledge of the FFTW object. You can always use `pyfftw.interfaces` if you want a quick speed-up, but be aware you'll potentially take a speed hit.

[Here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/15165621/709852) is a StackOverflow answer I wrote that might help with your problem. SO is a better place to ask this sort of question than github (which is intended for issues with pyfftw itself). I don't think you need to use cython to get the speed up you want - it significantly adds to the complexity if your software so unless you're using it already it's best to avoid it.

I also suggest reading the tutorial carefully to understand how pyfftw works.

Closing because this isn't an issue with pyfftw AFAICT and SO is a better forum for the question. I suggest re-asking there (I'll be notified if you tag it with pyfftw).
As to badges, put that in issue ( https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/issues/220 ). It feels a bit out of scope for this PR even if they are interesting.

https://github.com/dask/dask-ml/pull/228 has the non-memory impacting changes.

Overall, with just #228, things look really good. I'm able to cluster larger than memory datasets in low memory. This makes the changes here to manage memory & chunk size less important.

One potential remaining slowdown is how we handle reordering the large `X`. The approximation algorithm requires us to re-order `X` into two "blocks" (not dask blocks), a small, exactly computed block and a large approximated block:

```
[
    X_small,
    X_large,
]
```

Currently, this induces a slice with a large array of integers (which has also been recently improved in dask). But we're getting some performance warnings because the slice isn't *entirely* sorted. There are `n_components` that are out of place (something like 5-100 elements of the large `n_samples`). Looking into ways to improve this.

FWIW the desire to know chunk size of unknown arrays is a common enough problem it's worth solving correctly and generally IMHO. Issue ( https://github.com/dask/dask/issues/3293 ) has some discussion along these lines. The open question is the API that we provide. Feedback in that issue would be welcome.

If your C++ side is using Eigen arrays and your Python side is using numpy arrays, all you should need is an `#include <pybind11/eigen.h>` to get transparent conversion between them.  You can even get mutable references both ways (with some caveats--see [the docs on the Eigen support](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/cast/eigen.html)).

Casting to/from stl containers like `std::vector` similarly requires a `#include <pybind11/stl.h>`.
I don't think you absolutely need the latest version. It's just that the default (oldest) one doesn't invoke all the callbacks in a way that is needed for pybind11, which causes a crash.
Could this be done with a pointer constructor? `slice(ssize_t* start_, ssize_t* stop_, ssize_t* step_)` where `nullptr` is `None`? Or a C++17 optional interface might be nice.

I'm not keen on moving the (cmake + make)-built pybind11 headers from `<top-level>/include` to `<top-level>/.../site-.../include`, but if that's a distutils constraint then, in the interests of consistency, it's not a problem. Likewise with conda recipes, this just makes "python x.x" a build requirement as well as a run requirement, so there'll be identical (except for install site-pkg dir) `py27`, `py35` packages.

But what is the need to shift pybind11Config into site-pkgs? Distutils, I think, doesn't care about it unless to build the conda pkg, and one can use cmake for that step. Switching conda envs from 2.7 to 3.5 would force switching out the pybind11_py27 package for the 35, so Config would be in the same python-indep place and able to point to the correct py-dep headers location.

I guess you really need to know the C++ type to access the pybind11 internal type record, so ``dtype::register_implicit_conversion()`` would need to be a template function that is parameterized by that.

As far as I can tell, `detail::error_string()` needs `handle`, `str` and `cast` (and more than just forward declarations), while all of those in turn need `error_already_set`. I'm not really sure there is a way to move it up there other than to do the `std::string` cast manually using the raw C API (but that would just duplicate existing code).

1.) ExternalProjects come in handy if you have very different packages with different building mechanisms, build flags, different languages, etc. Unfortunately, this often happens in science (for example, the external project may run a code generator first). In addition, it can help keep projects separate during the build, keeping them from inheriting incompatible build flags, etc.

2.) `pybind11_add_module` is handy for some projects, but unconventional, and somewhat inflexible and prescriptive. For example, we may not what IPO or we might want to use a different (but compatible) C++ standard.

This feels like pybind11 is becoming the top project, whereas it really should be treated as a header library. Also, if there was another library that had its own function like add_module, a project couldn't use both.

A simpler approach would be to fix the error message with an error about `Base` being an unregistered class.  That, however, would require letting the argument loader communicate failures back to the dispatcher, as I roughly outlined in https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/issues/864#issuecomment-302407868

Right now the biggest issue in call failures is that we have no way to communicate *why* a call failed.  Sometimes you can look at the "invoked with" and see that it doesn't match, but having each overload communicate a failure reason would be pretty nice.

````
    if enc == 'Plaintext':

    elif enc == 'NT':

        hash.update(salt)
        hash_encoded = encodestring(hash.digest() + salt)
        password_renewed = hash_encoded.decode('utf-8')[:-1]
        password_renewed = '{%s}%s' % (enc, password_renewed)
    elif enc == 'SHA-512':
        password_renewed = sha512(new_value.encode('utf-8')).digest()
        password_renewed = '{%s}%s' % (enc, encodestring(password_renewed).decode('utf-8')[:-1])
    elif enc == 'SSHA-512':
        salt = urandom(4)
        hash = sha512(new_value.encode('utf-8'))
        hash.update(salt)
        hash_encoded = encodestring(hash.digest() + salt)
        password_renewed = hash_encoded.decode('utf-8')[:-1]
        password_renewed = '{%s}%s' % (enc, password_renewed)
    elif 'PKCS5S2':
        return atlassian_pbkdf2_sha1.encrypt(new_value)
    elif enc == 'CRYPT':
        password_renewed = crypt.crypt(new_value, crypt.mksalt(crypt.METHOD_CRYPT))
        password_renewed = '{%s}%s' % (enc, password_renewed)
    elif enc == 'CRYPT-MD5':
        # this worked too
        # return ldap_md5_crypt.encrypt(new_value)
        password_renewed = crypt.crypt(new_value, crypt.mksalt(crypt.METHOD_MD5))
        password_renewed = '{CRYPT}%s' % (password_renewed)
    elif enc == 'CRYPT-SHA-256':
        password_renewed = crypt.crypt(new_value, crypt.mksalt(crypt.METHOD_SHA256))
        password_renewed = '{CRYPT}%s' % (password_renewed)
    elif enc == 'CRYPT-SHA-512':
        password_renewed = crypt.crypt(new_value, crypt.mksalt(crypt.METHOD_SHA512))
        password_renewed = '{CRYPT}%s' % (password_renewed)
    return password_renewed
````

Our logging code uses the the [logging](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html) module in the Python standard library. This module defines 5 [logging levels](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging-levels) which we also use in our code.

The benefit of using and conforming to the practices in this module is that the logging level set with `-v` also applies to messages logged from other packages. For example, if you run the client with `-vv`, you will see messages from the `requests` package in the log output. If `-vv` isn't included, these messages aren't shown to the user because `requests` only logs messages with the level debug or info (unless errors occur with the request).

This also applies for other packages which my use the `acme` module. By using the standard logging levels, other programs can use `acme` but control the amount of logging information provided in a standardized way.

By default, we set the logging level to warning, meaning that messages logged as warning, error, or critical are shown to the user. HTTP bodies, as well as a lot of other output, is set to debug. We could define our own logging level below debug for HTTP bodies, but given the benefits of using `logging` in the standard way, I'm not sure that we should, especially as the change would be in `acme`.

You're right that the integration tests could just use `-vv`. I suspect the extra Vs were probably added in case we ever did something like change the default logging level.

This would be a great feature to have, I agree. But it's a ways away. Zip is both a compression algorithm and an archiver i.e. has standards for how it combines multiple files and directories into a single zip file. lz4 as currently implemented isn't an archiver, it's just compression. So, we'd need to standardize a way of having multiple files in a single file. Immediately one could think of some possibilities:

1. Simply using tar to create an archive, and then lz4 that (library.tar.lz4).

2. Combining mutliple files into a file, compressing one file per frame. This would need a catalogue ahead of the frames with the offsets for each file/frame. And some way to track file metadata (ownership, ctime, atime etc).

There may be prior art out there for option (2), I haven't looked. 

I'll leave this open, as it should be on the eventual roadmap. But ahead of that I want to finish the work implementing frame compression, and providing interfaces similar to those for zip etc in the standard library.

Yes, I just wanted to point the issue with the build: a context is required for placeholder (to access the current theme) and jinja expose 2 ways to pass the context:
- a `@contextfilter` decorator for filters which only pass context when used as a filter
- a `@contextfunction` decorator for function which only pass context when used as a function

Yes, we agree this needs a signature change, but the tricky part is not the signature change but how to feed the first context parameter.

*Ideal case 1*: we use these functions as both template filters and global functions but hopefully having both `@contextfilter` and `@contextfunction` on a same function is working in both case.
*Case 2*: we only use these functions as filters => we remove the `@front.app_template_global()` and keep the `@contextfilter` and the `@front.app_template_filter()`.
*Case 3*: we only use these functions as global functions => we remove the `@front.app_template_filter()` and keep the `@contextfunction` and the `@front.app_template_global()`.
*Worst case 4*: we use these functions as both template filters and global functions but having both `@contextfilter` and `@contextfunction` on a same function is not working => we either need to split these function in 2 separates ones or ensure we are only using template function or filter, not both.

Since the main goal is to not overstress or bring down the head nodes,  I did a little more research on rsync. It looks like there might be some options for various versions of rsync that might help us in that regard.   

If run as a daemon, some versions have a option --bwlimit=KBPS  which limits the I/O bandwidth used by the command in KBytes per second.  

There's also a few other options listed on this page
http://andre.ws/blog/posts/reducing-load-caused-by-rsync
1) rsyncing whole files (without using diff) - speeds up transfer and reduces cpu load
2) There's also an option for changing the encryption method used by the command which might increase the transfer rate on remote transfers and reduce the cpu load on the transferring server.  
3) We might also be able to use the nice command to change the execution priority of the rsync jobs so that other processes would have higher priority.

> How to modify plugin from another github fork to adapt it to work with streamlink?

Depends on the changes of the fork. You have to ask the maintainer(s) of the fork or fix it yourself. In cases when a project gets forked and something gets changed, it is not up the maintainers of the upstream project (Streamlink in this case) to fix or support any of the changes being made by the fork. Since Streamlink is being licenced under the 'BSD 2-Clause "Simplified" License', you can see and follow all of the changes in the git log.

> Is Streamlink checking each and every plugin for being politically correct after starting itself and only after that running plugin that I actually want to use ?
> If so, does my CPU has to "think" about 196 more plugins just to see if they are politically correct or not?

Yes, the `ethics` module gets loaded first and then it checks each plugin for being politically correct.
On a more serious note, since you actually mean whether each plugin file has to be free of any errors when it gets loaded, yes, this is the case:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.14.2/src/streamlink/session.py#L447-L486
As you can see, there is no try/catch block and this is intended behavior.

> Does it also mean that you access SSD once for each plugin ( 196 times ) just for the sake of
checking each plugin

The input URL has to be tested against all supported plugins until one matches. The impact shouldn't be that big though, since each plugin is usually pre-compiled (which means there's no additional parse/compile step) and depending on Python's optimizations of [`pkgutil.iter_modules`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pkgutil.html#pkgutil.iter_modules) and your OS's file system, there won't be X individual read accesses on the HDD / SSD / etc.

This whole process could be improved though by loading each plugin separately and immediately testing it until one matches. The [`--plugin-dir`](https://streamlink.github.io/cli.html#cmdoption-plugin-dirs) parameter could also be changed to override the default plugins dir constant instead of adding a new dir, but that would be a breaking change:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.14.2/src/streamlink_cli/main.py#L732-L736

One problem we have the current versioning system is that we really have at least three different things we need to version in the jupyter-js-widgets package:

1. The version of the widget protocol that the widget manager speaks.
2. The version of the model specification for the core widgets (i.e., the attributes and what they mean).
3. The version of the views (this also impacts the models, since the views can send a custom message that will be received on the python side).

We could simplify the view version to just be the model version the view understands. In order to do that, we really need the model to encapsulate *all* messages that can go between the kernel and frontend, which we could do by making everything go through the state attributes (for example, for button clicks, a new button state attribute that is number of times clicked).

```py

t = threading.Thread(target=func)

out = ipywidgets.Output()

with out:
```

At first glance, it looks like the webpack configuration for nglview doesn't include a CSS loader, so webpack presumably doesn't know what to do with it. At the top of `webpack.config.js`, loaders is defined as:

```
var loaders = [
    { test: /\.json$/, loader: 'json-loader' },
]; 
```

If you want to require CSS, you need a CSS loader. The most common way would be to change this to:

```
var loaders = [
    { test: /\.json$/, loader: 'json-loader' },
    { test: /\.css$/, loader: 'style-loader!css-loader' }
]; 
```

You'll also need to update the dev dependencies in package.json to include `style-loader` and `css-loader`.

I was wrong, actually the stdlib `ssl` module's IDN handling is just completely broken; basically it's just impossible to connect to IDNs using `ssl`. Not only does it insist on decoding SNI using IDNA2003, but if you pass in `server_hostname=` as a pre-encoded byte string then it will first decode it using IDNA 2003 and then re-encode it using 2003, so `ssl_object.server_hostname` is always a U-label. Furthermore, the `match_hostname` logic then compares this U-label *directly against the A-labels in the certificate* (!!!!!), which will never work (except perhaps [accidentally via the CN field](https://lwn.net/Comments/); this is that thing where the CN field can contain anything which is a security bug so they're being removed, but in particular they sometimes contain the U-label because why not).

This is not something we can fix; as long as `ssl` works like this, and we use `ssl`, we just can't handle IDNA in `ssl`. I guess we could give a nicer error message if someone tries?

What we can do now is switch to using IDNA 2008 correctly in `getaddrinfo`, so at least if someone tries to connect to `https://faß.de` then they'll go to the right place, and then fail the certificate check, instead of going to the wrong place and passing the certificate check. (And also go to the right place if they try `http://faß.de`.)

And in the longer run, I guess either `ssl` needs to get fixed (maybe 3.7?), or we need to switch to PyOpenSSL (but I'm *very* nervous about this because that means taking on a ton of really tricky responsibilities), or maybe PEP 543 will save us all.

I'm leaning towards `run` / `run_sync`, but this has the downside that `run_in_trio_thread` is used both before and after, with different meanings, so a normal deprecation doesn't work. We could just rip off the band-aid, but maybe we should take this opportunity to make another change. I've noticed it's kind of weird and annoying to have to call both `current_run_in_trio_thread` and `current_run_sync_in_trio_thread` and keep track of both returned values; if you want to call trio APIs from a thread you'll probably need both. So maybe we should define a concept of a ReentryVehicle: an object that provides `run` and `run_sync` methods that are appropriate to where-ever you're launching from. So it'd be like:

```python
in_trio = trio.current_blocking_reentry_vehicle()
# then in your thread
in_trio.run(...)
```

And you can imagine providing similar vehicles to reenter from asyncio or twisted or whatever.

...for asyncio / twisted / etc., it also makes sense to have trio→them reentry vehicles. Might want to keep that in mind when naming things: an asyncio→trio vehicle is no the same as a trio→asyncio vehicle.

(If an API design allows for an awesomely punny name then it's probably a good idea. I'm pretty sure that's in the zen of python somewhere.)

In general this is not very extensible, however, since it requires one to know which modules will be imported by each shared module ahead of time (before even starting the interpreter). But in principle these shared modules could load any different module, even dependent on what happens at run-time.

This means that as soon as we have shared modules (an absolute must to get `numpy` working with multiple `Jep` instances) we can no longer be truly sure our `except Foo:` statements will actually work.
